pattern: Two ants that bounce off each other are indistinguishable from two ants that pass straight through each other, so the last ant to fall is just whichever single ant -- ignoring collisions entirely -- has the farthest lone walk to its own edge.
hints: Picture two ants meeting and reversing direction: swap their identities instead, and the set of positions occupied over time is exactly the same as if they had walked through one another.
Once collisions are irrelevant, each left-moving ant simply needs `position` seconds to reach 0, and each right-moving ant needs `n - position` seconds to reach n.

The last moment anything is still on the plank is the maximum of all those independent travel times -- take the max over the left list of their positions and over the right list of n minus their positions.

edge_inputs.description: statement-style mixed group with a collision in the middle
smallest plank, single ant already at the edge
smallest plank, single ant already at the far edge
one ant already sitting at each end, both fall immediately
ants exactly at the midpoint moving toward each other, forcing a mid-plank swap
left group closer to falling than right group, but right group has the farthest single walker

stress.description: many ants scattered across a long plank split randomly between directions
stress.note: positions are drawn without repeats across left+right, matching the uniqueness constraint
